coreConcepts: Kingdom of God / Kingdom of Heaven

passages.text: Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness: for they shall be filled. Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God. Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God. Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness' sake: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
passages.context: The opening of the Sermon on the Mount, redefining blessedness away from worldly prosperity toward inner and relational virtues.

passages.text: Ye are the salt of the earth: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be salted? it is thenceforth good for nothing, but to be cast out, and to be trodden under foot of men. Ye are the light of the world. A city that is set on an hill cannot be hid.
passages.context: Jesus tells his followers they are already the salt and light of the world — their nature is to illumine and preserve, not to hide.

passages.text: Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.
passages.context: The practical consequence of being light — visible action that points beyond the self to the source.

passages.text: But I say unto you, That ye resist not evil: but whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also. And if any man will sue thee at the law, and take away thy coat, let him have thy cloak also. And whosoever shall compel thee to go a mile, go with him twain. Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee turn not thou away.
passages.context: The teaching of non-retaliatory love, inverting the logic of power with the logic of abundance and disarming generosity.

passages.text: But I say unto you, Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you, and persecute you; That ye may be the children of your Father which is in heaven: for he maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust.
passages.context: The most radical ethical demand in the Sermon: love of enemy as the mark of divine kinship, grounded in the Father's impartial generosity.

passages.text: Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect.
passages.context: The summary of the sermon's ethical vision: completeness in love, not flawless rule-keeping.

passages.text: After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil.
passages.context: The model prayer Jesus gives, centering on alignment with divine will, forgiveness as a two-directional flow, and trust in daily provision.

passages.text: Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal: But lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust doth corrupt, and where thieves do not break through nor steal: For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.
passages.context: The heart's gravitational pull — what we treasure, we become; inner orientation determines the whole life.

passages.text: Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they toil not, neither do they spin: And yet I say unto you, That even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these. Wherefore, if God so clothe the grass of the field, which to day is, and to morrow is cast into the oven, shall he not much more clothe you, O ye of little faith?
passages.context: The argument from natural beauty against anxiety: creation sustains itself by trust, not striving — an invitation to childlike presence.

passages.text: But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you. Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof.
passages.context: The resolution of the anxiety teaching: radical present-moment orientation, grounded in the kingdom as primary reality.

passages.text: Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you: For every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened.
passages.context: The promise of responsive relationship: asking, seeking, and knocking as postures of active trust, met with guaranteed response.

passages.text: Why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother's eye, but considerest not the beam that is in thine own eye? Or how wilt thou say to thy brother, Let me pull out the mote out of thine eye; and, behold, a beam is in thine own eye? Thou hypocrite, first cast out the beam out of thine own eye; and then shalt thou see clearly to cast out the mote out of thy brother's eye.
passages.context: Self-knowledge must precede and enable genuine perception of others — perception is always first self-perception.

passages.text: Verily I say unto you, Except ye be converted, and become as little children, ye shall not enter into the kingdom of heaven. Whosoever therefore shall humble himself as this little child, the same is greatest in the kingdom of heaven.
passages.context: The radical inversion of greatness: the child who has no status or self-importance becomes the model of kingdom belonging.

passages.text: But many that are first shall be last; and the last shall be first.
passages.context: The kingdom's logic of reversal — status, power, and priority are all transposed in divine economy.

passages.text: For even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many.
passages.context: The master states that the purpose of his life is downward service — the greatest is slave of all.

passages.text: And one of them, which was a lawyer, asked him a question, tempting him, and saying, Master, which is the great commandment in the law? Jesus said unto him, Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind. This is the first and great commandment. And the second is like unto it, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself. On these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets.
passages.context: The entire teaching condensed into two movements: vertical love of God and horizontal love of neighbor, each needing the other.

passages.text: And he said, A certain man had two sons: And the younger of them said to his father, Father, give me the portion of goods that falleth to me. And he divided unto them his living. And not many days after the younger son gathered all together, and took his journey into a far country, and there wasted his substance with riotous living. And when he came to himself, he said, How many hired servants of my father's have bread enough and to spare, and I perish with hunger! I will arise and go to my father, and will say unto him, Father, I have sinned against heaven, and before thee. And he arose, and came to his father. But when he was yet a great way off, his father saw him, and had compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him.
passages.context: The defining image of divine welcome — the father who runs before the son can finish his speech of contrition.

passages.text: The kingdom of God is like to a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and sowed in his field: Which indeed is the least of all seeds: but when it is grown, it is the greatest among herbs, and becometh a tree, so that the birds of the air come and lodge in the branches thereof.
passages.context: The kingdom grows not through power and spectacle but through the almost invisible — the smallest seed becomes shelter for all.

passages.text: Neither shall they say, Lo here! or, lo there! for, behold, the kingdom of God is within you.
passages.context: The kingdom is not a future location to be sought but an interior reality already present — the most radical of all his kingdom sayings.

passages.text: Jesus said unto her, I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live: And whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die. Believest thou this?
passages.context: One of the seven I AM sayings in John, spoken at the tomb of Lazarus — death is not final for those joined to the life-source.

passages.text: Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me. If ye had known me, ye should have known my Father also: and from henceforth ye know him, and have seen him.
passages.context: The central I AM declaration of John's Gospel: Jesus as the living path rather than a set of instructions about a path.

passages.text: A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another. By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples, if ye have love one to another.
passages.context: Love is not just a rule but the identifying mark, the very face of the community — how the world will know.

passages.text: Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends.
passages.context: The definition of love at its apex: not sentiment but voluntary surrender of life itself for another.

passages.text: And Jesus, when he was baptized, went up straightway out of the water: and, lo, the heavens were opened unto him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove, and lighting upon him: And lo a voice from heaven, saying, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.
passages.context: The moment of divine affirmation before any public ministry — belovedness precedes and grounds all action.

passages.text: Then said Jesus, Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do. And they parted his raiment, and cast lots.
passages.context: The prayer from the cross — the teaching of enemy love enacted at the moment of its ultimate test, mercy flowing even in death.
